data.title: JCPB: Much Improved Fixed Income ETF From JPMorgan
data.description:
Thesis

We first wrote about the JPMorgan Core Plus Bond ETF (JCPB) three years ago when we did not like the fund's analytics and preferred a long position in another JPM fund, namely the JPMorgan Income ETF (JPIE). A lot has changed since, with JCPB finding its stride and posting attractive results, prompting us to revisit the name and re-rate it.

What Does the Fund Do?

The ETF is a multi-asset one that:

Expands opportunities for returns and income by combining a broad foundation of high-quality core bonds with dynamic sector allocation and a macro overlay. Draws on the best ideas of the entirety of J.P. Morgan’s fixed Income platform across a wide investable universe. Integrates bottom-up security selection with top-down analysis improves potential for attractive risk-adjusted returns and increased income.

Active funds have the ability to pivot from overpriced segments of the market into underpriced ones and generally position a fixed income portfolio to align it with the current macro conditions.

The fund is currently overweight in treasuries and corporate bonds.

Treasuries represent 37% of the portfolio, followed by corporate bonds at 24% and Agency MBS securities at 22%. The current build is substantially overweight in investment-grade assets, which make up most of the portfolio:

The fund only contains 12.4% in below investment grade assets, mainly in the double-BB bucket. The rest of the holdings are investment grade, with a 10% not-rated bucket. This build ensures rates are the main risk factor, followed by credit spreads.

The ETF is very granular, containing over 2,000 names, and comes with a 6.1-year duration. Additional analytics are as follows:

  • AUM: $11 billion
  • Expense ratio: 0.38%
  • SEC yield: 4.7%
  • Portfolio YTM: 5.22%

The ETF has a very sizable AUM of $11 billion and comes with a 4.7% SEC yield. Do remember that when looking at an active fixed income ETF, one has to consider a total return framework since yield is just a part of the equation. In our performance analysis below, we use such a metric. The ETF falls in the intermediate duration bucket and has been able to post very good risk/reward analytics in the past year. Let us look closer.

Performance and Risk

The fund has done well in the past year, posting total returns in line with a cohort of peers:

We are building a cohort of peers composed of the JPMorgan Income ETF (JPIE), the PIMCO Multisector Bond Active ETF (PYLD), and the TCW Flexible Income ETF (FLXR). All of the above are multi-sector active fixed income ETFs, but they do come with different duration profiles.

PYLD is the clear outperformer, followed by JCPB and FLXR. JPIE comes in last because it has a very short duration, thus representing a very conservative choice.

JCPB has achieved its total return profile with the following volatility analytics as posted by the Seeking Alpha platform:

The ETF's standard deviation is 3.59%, and its annualized volatility is 3.9%. These are fairly low figures when the returns and duration are considered. Let us look at drawdowns as well:

The name had poor downside management and a less than stellar portfolio in 2024, when it recorded the highest drawdown from the cohort at -4%. Things improved in 2025 and 2026, with the ETF's drawdown in line with the peer group. Do remember this is an active ETF, and managers can adjust holdings and portfolios to make them more resilient. Going back to the comparison to JPIE, that JPM fund has a very low duration and very low drawdowns, as we can see from the graph above. We like the fact that JCPB has been able to manage its downside better, because this is a cornerstone of a robust fund going forward.

Forward Macro Take

We are of the opinion that well-run multi-sector fixed income funds are set for a couple of banner years given the current macro. While we are in an environment where the Fed is still cutting rates, the macro has been volatile with inflation worries coming back:

Higher inflation expectations have pushed rates higher, thus putting pressure on fixed-income assets, even in the intermediate-duration bucket. This can present an opportunity for active managers since MBS securities in particular have become very attractive. A swift end to the Iran war can bring rate cuts back, which in turn can result in outsized returns for intermediate fixed income.

While passive funds can only navigate in a static fashion what the market gives them, active names like JCPB can trade expectations and overbought/oversold conditions. We are of the opinion that rates are now overextended to the upside, and intermediate-duration funds like JCPB can provide for very robust returns for the rest of the year if inflation expectations normalize.

Conclusion

JCPB is an active multi-sector fixed income ETF from JPMorgan. The fund is able to change its portfolio as it sees fit, and we have seen a massive improvement in downside risk management in the past year and a half. After poor duration management in late 2024/early 2025, the ETF has done a much better job in the past year, posting downside risk similar to a cohort of peers. With rates overextended to the upside and a healthy SEC yield, the ETF can post total returns in excess of 7% this year with an intermediate duration build. If the market prices in more Fed cuts, the fund can get a significant boost. We like the changes made to the JCPB portfolio and are a 'Buy' here.

data.title: ServiceNow Earnings Selloff: 3 Reasons The Market Got It Wrong
data.description:
In my view, ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW) represents one of those "unrepeatable" investment opportunities available during periods of panic or chaos, such as we have witnessed over the past few months. The recent extreme price drop in ServiceNow stock reflects the same fear-driven panic that we have seen throughout the Software industry. While the company reported a mixed quarter, I am skeptical about the magnitude of the negative impact reflected in the share price drop.

At first glance, the quarterly results appear to have been disastrous. Although the post-market showed a 14% decline in shares after the earnings release, I do not think that reflects the actual impact of the quarter's results. To me, the market's reaction appears to be far more severe than the quarterly results would suggest.

There are two key areas where I think the market may have overreacted to the quarterly results. First, the company made only a small adjustment to its margin. Second, while certain large transactions were delayed due to geopolitical uncertainty in the Middle East, I consider these issues to be temporary. I will address these items in greater detail in the next paragraphs.

By the way, what is important to understand now is that the selloff in ServiceNow's stock appears to be largely driven by market-wide concerns regarding the Software sector, rather than by fundamental weaknesses within ServiceNow. The market punished ServiceNow as though it represented a systemic weakness in the business model when in reality, the underlying metrics continue to reflect strong demand for the product, solid execution from management and continued growth in enterprise relationships through use of the ServiceNow platform.

Therefore, viewing this downward pressure on ServiceNow's stock price as evidence that the Company is structurally vulnerable to replacement by AI-based alternatives also strikes me as an oversimplification. Without revealing too many details now, I can say that the quarterly results suggest just the opposite — i.e., that large customers perceive ServiceNow as an increasingly essential component of their technology platforms and less likely to be supplanted by alternative solutions.

As always, thank you for taking the time to read and as always, please let me know your thoughts in the comments. Let's create a space for mutual insight.

The Fears Surrounding Artificial Intelligence and The Software Massacre

This year, there has been quite an amount of concern in regards to how artificial intelligence may negatively affect the whole software business, particularly as many software businesses are taking a beating on Wall Street. OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO) have both made tremendous advancements in their latest versions of advanced models. As such, these developments have raised fears for some investors who fear that artificial intelligence could potentially take over much of what software currently does; this would lead to automated workflow processes being completed at a much faster pace than ever before and a drastic reduction of the total number of seats that can be sold to other companies.

In regards to ServiceNow, since January, the company's share price has dropped by roughly 33 percent. Additionally, if the current trend continues, ServiceNow will experience its seventh straight month of decline, making it one of the longest streaks of monthly declines that ServiceNow has experienced in recent years.

Ahead of its quarterly earnings report, ServiceNow faced substantial pressure from the market. The company needed to show both healthy financial performance, as well as high levels of interest or demand for its new AI based products. The market demanded evidence and factual data rather than just empty promise.

In my previous article titled "Software Is 2026's Strongest Buy Opportunity: Here's Why," I provided specific examples of the future winners and losers of the AI revolution in software. Specifically, I mentioned that ServiceNow was going to be a clear winner of the AI revolution in software. Today’s numbers (excluding the extreme volatility caused by the market reaction), confirm my previous premise.

Earnings

In my view, the 14% drop in after-market trading was a fear-driven reaction. The evidence for this is quite simple, two things drove the market’s reaction. On the one hand, the Gross Margin, has declined from 83% in Q2 2024 to 79.5% in Q1 2026.

On the other hand, the time it takes to close big contracts has been delayed due to the war in the Middle East. Both of these are legitimate issues, however both need to be placed in perspective. As I previously stated, the Company's own explanation was that there is additional pressure on margins due to the Armis integration, and that this is a short-term issue rather than a structural one. Likewise, when we discuss Deals, we are discussing timing vs. reduced demand. If the Business had really deteriorated, then it would have been hard to see such a sharp increase in Annual Guidance.

On the other hand, the results were very good. Total Revenues exceeded estimates at $3.77 Billion.

Subscription Revenue exceeded Estimates at $3.67 Billion. More importantly, the Company increased its FY26 subscription revenue guidance to $15.74-15.78 Billion, clearly beating market expectations.

Finally, demand remained strong: cRPO was up 22.5% year-over-year with incredible stability over the past eight quarters.

Transactions greater than $5 Million in Net New ACV increased nearly 80%. Further, the customer base continued to grow which indicates large customers are spending more money on the platform not less.

The other significant factor the markets have largely overlooked: the profit picture is improving. Adjusted EPS rose for the second straight quarter from $0.63 in Q2 '24 to $0.97 in Q1 '26.

That represents an approximate 20% increase relative to Q1 '25. In short: despite increasing investment and some near term noise, ServiceNow continues to earn more money every quarter. This doesn't look like a company losing its way; this looks like a scalable platform continuing to grow.

This is why the current stock price appears so out of whack with the fundamental value of the company. While the overall software space fell dramatically over the last few quarters, and therefore ServiceNow (as a representative of that industry) also fell, the reaction post-results implied an operating issue existed. Instead, ServiceNow reported revenue above expectations, raised guidance, and still showed very strong demand.

In essence, as you can see, the business fundamentals were solid again, but the stock price took most of the damage due to emotion. And that is exactly what creates such a disconnect between how well the business performed vs. how low the stock price fell.

Why the Stock Price Was Wrong: Three Points

1. Margin Reductions Are Short-Term; They’re Also Small

While the reduction in the forecasted subscription gross margin rate from 82% to 81.5% caused a stir in the marketplace (it’s really about $79 million added to ServiceNow’s estimated $15.76 billion in subscription revenues in fiscal year 26) the effect on ServiceNow’s overall profitability will be small. In fact, ServiceNow has made it clear that the margin reductions are primarily a result of the integration of Armis, and as such they represent nothing more than a short-term disruption to the service provider’s business model. That’s a key distinction because while the margin impacts do create obstacles for ServiceNow’s bottom line, they are not indicative of a long-term structural flaw in the company’s business model.

That said, let’s quantify what we’re talking about here. For fiscal year 26, ServiceNow is projected to produce approximately $5.74 billion in free cash flow.

Therefore, $79 million represents roughly 1.4 percent of annualized free cash flows. However, after today’s news event (a 14% decline in the stock price resulting in a loss of approximately $15 billion in total market capitalization), I believe the market reacted much more aggressively than warranted by the economic implications relative to the size of this company.

2. Delays in Closing Large Contracts Represent Timing Issues Not Demand Issues

ServiceNow stated that without the contract delays associated with ongoing conflicts in the Middle East, the Company believes its subscription revenue growth would have been approximately 1 percentage point higher during the quarter.

As we’ve done previously, let’s convert the Company’s data into some simple numbers to better understand what ServiceNow is trying to say. One percentage point of $3.67 billion (subscription revenue) equals approximately $37 million. When we use those numbers to describe the difference between expected and actual performance, it becomes apparent that we are dealing with a situation where deals are being closed late, not cancelled. To me, that sounds like what happens when there are geopolitical issues affecting how quickly large enterprise customers can make decisions.

However, that does not mean that ServiceNow did not see an increase in demand for its services. Although ServiceNow had big delays in closing many of its larger deals, the Company still increased its fiscal year 26 subscription revenue guidance by approximately $200 million ($15.74-15.78 billion vs. $15.54 billion consensus). Given that ServiceNow significantly exceeded market expectations for both quarterly and annual subscription revenue growth, it seems reasonable to conclude that deals aren’t going away; they’re simply taking longer to close. ServiceNow provided similar guidance regarding fiscal year 26 quarterly subscription revenue performance ($3.815 –$3.82 billion), which also exceeds current market expectations. It appears that ServiceNow is attempting to communicate that all of the recent deal delays experienced are merely timing-related issues and not evidence of a broader decline in demand for its products and services.

3. Actual Demand Metrics Have Been Improving, Not Deteriorating

When reviewing actual demand metrics for ServiceNow (e.g., CRPO), we get a different story. CRPO was $12.64 billion in the quarter, representing a year-over-year increase of 22.5%, and a year-over-year constant currency increase of 21%. Additionally, CRPO has consistently grown within a range of 20.5%-22.5% over the last eight quarters. That type of steady-state demand behavior doesn’t appear consistent with a rapidly declining business.

Another interesting metric: ServiceNow signed 16 deals greater than $5 million in net new ACV in Q1 26, representing an increase of almost 80% versus prior year. While it might be easy to argue that a “better-than-expected” number like this could simply reflect AI “disrupting” the business model for large enterprise software providers or customers reducing spending on IT infrastructure, I think it’s difficult to argue against either scenario based upon this particular metric. Rather, it suggests that large enterprises are continuing to commit to using more of ServiceNow’s technology platforms.

In addition, ServiceNow ended Q1 ’26 with 630 customers spending more than $5 million annually in ACV. This should be viewed as further evidence that large enterprise customers are increasing their commitment to ServiceNow’s platform, rather than decreasing it.

Target Price

The correct way to look at this is not from where we are now, but rather how big could the earnings be by FY28 when we see all of the money ServiceNow has invested in AI start to come out in the P&L. This is why I am using FY28 to do this analysis.

From there, the consensus among analysts as reported on Bloomberg estimates adjusted EPS of $6.015 in FY28.

From there, a reasonable multiple based upon the type of business and growth rate would be applied. A multiple of 27x seems reasonable for ServiceNow given its growth expectation of revenue and earning rates of around 18-20 percent. Based upon this, I estimate ServiceNow will trade near $162 per share. Therefore, my target price was derived from nothing more than a formulaic methodology: future earnings x a reasonable multiple. The hardest part of this process is getting the market to eventually view ServiceNow through an entirely different lens. Rather than viewing AI as a risk to their model they need to ultimately understand it is what makes them more relevant.

Technical Analysis

(I will explain ServiceNow’s technical analysis relative to the April 22 close date, and not regarding the prices after the earnings release).

ServiceNow remains in a long-term downtrend; it is trading much closer to its 52-week low ($81.24) than its 52-week high ($211.48), which maintains a cautious stance overall. As of the day immediately before the release of its quarterly earnings, ServiceNow closed 4.6% higher than its 20-day simple moving average (SMA), however it was 18.2% lower than its 100-day SMA. The significant difference between these two SMAs suggests a possible short term rebound within a longer term declining trend.

The current moving averages create challenges for ServiceNow: the shorter 20-day moving average is under the longer 50-day moving average. In addition, a “Death Cross” occurred in August 2025 (when the 50-day moving average became less than the 200-day moving average) which suggests the long-term trend of ServiceNow has transitioned to a seller-friendly environment. However, the MACD (Moving Average Convergence Divergence) indicator, which is used to determine if there is upward or downward momentum/trends, is currently showing the MACD line greater than the signal line along with a positive histogram. Historically, this has indicated that downward pressure had diminished somewhat since the beginning of the price movement.

In terms of price movement, ServiceNow’s stock price has declined by approximately 33.08% during the last twelve months. This loss in value matches what has happened in other areas of the market related to how ServiceNow has been re-valued. With a total loss of approximately 33% during the past year, $98 becomes increasingly important as this is an area of concentration for buying activity in ServiceNow and represents a potential point in time when selling would begin to represent a failure of a bounce back into the downtrend.

The price movement in ServiceNow created a clear falling wedge formation which can be viewed as technically indicating bearish exhaustion and potential reversal to bullish. When a falling wedge forms and is accompanied by a positive divergence on the MACD, then it is often viewed as strong evidence of a reversal from bearish to bullish.

As ServiceNow’s stock price fell in value, the multiple service companies use to evaluate their stocks decreased dramatically. From over 64 times trailing EPS (Price per Earnings) in late 2024 down to just 23.28 times trailing EPS today, we believe paying almost 23 times trailing EPS for a software company that is growing at 22 percent per year and earning a profit margin of around 70% is statistically impossible in the technology space.

Conclusion

I think there’s been a loss of direction by investors regarding ServiceNow. While ServiceNow did not deliver a perfect quarter, that is not my concern. My concern is that the market has over-reacted to the structural problems I don't see evidenced in the numbers. Conversely, what I am seeing is a big rise in revenue (beat expectations), increasing annual estimates for future growth, increased size of contracts being signed and improving the service relationship ServiceNow has with Enterprise Clients. I also see temporary pressure on margins as a result of issues ServiceNow can identify and control. I see many of the large contract delays (not cancellations). Most importantly, I see a marketplace that immediately reacts to "AI" adjacent to software and assumes that all current players will be obsoleted. I find that to be a lazy approach. And wrong.

To put it simply, ServiceNow doesn't appear to be an AI-based platform that is being destroyed. Rather, ServiceNow's platform appears to be one that is going to become more central to its customer's use cases through the use of AI. Once the noise fades, the facts will still exist. Those facts tell a much different story than where the stock price currently sits.

That is why I maintain my Strong Buy rating (3% in my portfolio). Not because the last earnings report was perfect, but because once again, the market has confused panic with analysis.

data.title: Tesla Q1: Why I'm Constructive On The Narrative But The Stock Is Still A Sell
data.description:
2025 was not a good year for Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) from a financial perspective (check it in my last article). In fact, 2024 was not such a good year, either. The year in which there was a peak in automotive revenues was 2023, and since then the gross margin has been decreasing and automotive revenues as well, showing a certain loss of relevance in the market and evidencing that even though there is a bullish story behind it, Tesla is still a company that depends on the consumer discretionary sector.

In other words, Tesla is affected by competition (yes, BYD (BYDDF) is a problem in international markets), and is also impacted by the macroeconomic scenario.

All of this comes together with an extremely stretched valuation. Even after a correction year to date, the forward P/E is still close to 200x.

But don’t get me wrong. Tesla still has a large potential to capture with several optionalities. But this needs to be proven. Tesla needs to prove that it can scale full self-drive, or FSD, even further, develop advances in Optimus, proving that these businesses are actually viable and that it will have a large share of the total addressable market.

2026 is precisely the year that Tesla needs to prove this. Show traction in the optionalities and also show that the automotive segment will recover in the next quarters.

And well, Q1 surprised me very positively. Tesla showed me exactly what I wanted to see, which is more concrete data about these optionalities. It is still not something completely clear, but it has everything to become more mature in the next quarters.

Tesla’s Q1: Pretty Solid

Both Tesla’s Q1 top line and EPS were a beat. Revenue was only slightly above consensus, around a 0.8% surprise, largely because the market already had the delivery numbers. But EPS was a 17% surprise, but also driven by non-operational factors.

Automotive revenues increased 16%, which is a figure I already consider positive, but it is not so bullish considering that QoQ, this figure represents a decline. However, the services and other revenue segment came very strong, growing 42%. With this better mix, we also had a better gross margin, which shows factors such as higher efficiency, lower leasing in total deliveries and a positive impact from FSD growth.

I particularly liked this gross margin a lot, this helps remove the stigma that I mentioned at the beginning of the article, because previous numbers corroborate a narrative that pricing power is not as good as before, that the environment requires promotions and etc., and as Tesla rebuilds this margin, a bullish narrative can be rebuilt.

Going to EBITDA, the margin remained around 16%, but YoY it is a relevant expansion, making adjusted EBITDA grow 30%, and EPS grow 52% if adjusted.

But this “adjusted” part was precisely one of the points I did not like. Note that the difference between GAAP EPS and non-GAAP EPS was large and bigger than other quarters. Most of this difference is explained by an increase in stock-based compensation (SBC), which even though it is a non-cash expense, dilutes shareholders. SBC by itself is not a bad thing, it is a way to reward employees, but Tesla needs to prove that it is becoming more profitable in GAAP terms over time, that is, it needs to prove that operating leverage will also serve to keep this expense controlled.

Tesla: Bullish Story Became Stronger

Even though I found the numbers solid, the most bullish part is in the narrative that is becoming more concrete. In this presentation Tesla highlighted several operational advances that make it easier to envision that eventually segments like CyberCab and Optimus will become large revenue streams.

Cumulative deliveries advanced to 9.2 million, growing 21%, and active FSD subscriptions advanced to 1.28 million. These are very important numbers, because they help us make some calculations.

In this article, I made some calculations to see where each optionality could reach. My assumption was the ~10 million users that Tesla intends to reach, with $99/month, and thus it would be an incremental revenue of $12 billion (and remember that the margin here is much better, because it is a SaaS). With a 9.2 million installed base today, it is easy to imagine that in a few years Tesla could reach, for instance, 12 million, which with 50% penetration would already be 6 million FSD subscribers, an important milestone and one that already makes the ~10 million seem close.

But the Robotaxi part is where it really impressed me. Cumulative paid robotaxi miles have already surpassed 1,600,000 and now we have more mature information, including ramping unsupervised in some cities like Austin. Besides that, we have some bold information, such as the expectation that eventually the Cybercab will “replace the Model Y Fleet and will be the largest volume vehicle in the fleet.” By the way, both the Tesla Semi and the Cybercab are in “pilot production” status.

In addition, Optimus is with manufacturing capacity already being built, and also with expectations that it will eventually be produced on a larger scale.

Even though these are not exactly something like “production is already happening and is red-hot,” they are information that help build the narrative. Before Tesla said that the goal was 1 million robotaxis in circulation, now we already have production capacity, pilot production, many miles driven, and several cities already in early traction.

Therefore, I see this Q1 in a very positive way for all optionalities, and I believe that in the next quarters we can have even more concrete and interesting information.

Tesla’s Valuation: Buying Optionality

Tesla’s problem remains valuation. Even if we believe in all these new revenue streams, we are talking about a revenue of ~$109 billion and a total EBITDA of $27.9 billion.

Q1 adjusted EBITDA was $3.6 billion, annualized it is something close to $14 billion. Let’s round it to $45 billion with the potential growth of the core and margin improvement ($27.9 billion of optionalities +$14 billion today + some growth). Today Tesla is a $1.4 trillion market cap company (and enterprise value). If we believe that in some period between 2030 and 2035 it will be able to deliver ~$50 billion of EBITDA, we are still talking about multiples above 25x.

Bottom Line: I’m Still Avoiding, But I’m More Optimistic Now

Optionality is something that fundamentally you do not pay for. What I mean by this is, do you know about Amazon’s (AMZN) Zoox? It is a self-driving cars division. I believe that no analyst considers Zoox in Amazon’s fair value, not only because it is practically insignificant, but because by definition it is an optionality, if everything goes right, it comes as a positive surprise, but if it goes wrong, these numbers were not even in your Excel sheet.

In Tesla’s case it is the opposite. The only way to justify the valuation is to consider that all optionalities will work. Cybercab? Massive production, high market share, they will be able to deliver. Optimus? They will be able to scale this business in a viable way. FSD? They will have high penetration and the installed base will continue to grow. This is the only way to justify Tesla’s valuation.

And even if all these revenue streams are becoming more concrete, as I mentioned in my article, there are uncertainties in execution. Even if Tesla manages to deliver this, let’s assume it can only deliver it in 2040 or 2045. This changes the value a lot, because now, you are waiting 15-20 years to have a stock that trades at 25x adjusted EBITDA. Of course, if Tesla delivers all this in 2030, great, the valuation is justified. But my point here is that if there is any delay, more intense competition, if margins are slightly worse than initially expected, all of this erodes the margin of safety.

Therefore, even though my view is more constructive for Tesla as a company, as a stock I prefer to continue avoiding it until the scenario becomes clearer regarding the scale of these new ventures and also the valuation.

data.title: Travel + Leisure: Delinquency Fears Appear Overdone
data.description:
Shares of Travel + Leisure Co. (TNL) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining over 50% as the company has aggressively repurchased stock thanks to strong free cash flow. However, mixed results sent the stock plunging 13% on Wednesday. While the company delivered an EPS beat, there was disappointment it did not raise guidance, and concerns about credit quality have resurfaced.

I last covered shares in February, when I retained them as a “Buy” even as they eclipsed my original $70 price target. In hindsight, I got greedy pressing a winning trade, and shares are down 12% since then. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit TNL.

In the company’s first quarter, Travel + Leisure earned $1.45 per share, which beat estimates by $0.14 as revenue grew 3% to $961 million. Vacation ownership EBITDA grew 20% to $191 million as revenue grew 6%, which is strong and not something usually associated with a plunge in the stock. Volume per guest (“VPG”) was up 3% to $3,321 as tours increased by 5%, and net vacation ownership (“VOI”) sales were up 11%. Gross sales were up 7%, but the company set aside less for defaults, which made net sales growth stronger. Shares fell in a large part because investors are doubting that trend can be sustained.

Early-Stage Delinquencies Were A Negative Surprise

On the earnings call, management called out that early-stage delinquencies are starting to rise, especially in recent vintages. While not altering its loan loss provision guidance for this year, provisions could rise if this delinquency trend is sustained or more of these delinquencies extend out towards 90 days. Most of these loans were originated “over the last several quarters.” Later in the call, it said the issue arose from loans specifically in the past three quarters, and there was no particular attribute (i.e., income band or FICO score) driving it.

TNL has maintained fairly conservative underwriting standards in recent years, which makes this development surprising. In the quarter, new loans were originated to consumers with a 740 FICO score on average, an excellent level, with down payments over 20%. Given these factors, I would not expect loan losses to be especially problematic. It also cannot blame any oil price inflation, since that would not have materialized in time during Q1 for there to be delinquencies.

Provisions May Run A Bit Higher But Should Be Manageable

In general, consumer credit trends reported across financial companies were strong in Q1. Even credit card issuers like Synchrony (SYF) reported solid results, which makes TNL a surprising outlier; this fact likely exacerbated the negative reaction in shares on Wednesday. With stable employment, a solid tax refund season, and strong credit quality, I struggle to see this turning into a genuine default cycle and am inclined to see it like management as a “wobble.”

In Q1, it provisioned at a 19% rate, which was in line with the Q4 provision. For the full year 2025, the provision rate was 20.7%, so this was still a bit better. A continuation of delinquencies threatens the progress, which ultimately limits how much money it can collect from securitizing its VOI receivables. Elevated down payment levels should limit loss given default, which supports a lower provisioning rate. I see a definite risk that provisions rise from the 19% rate, but I would be surprised to see it rise to 21% or higher, absent a clearer downturn in the economy.

Its Travel and Membership unit saw revenue decline $15 million to $165 million, with EBITDA down 13% to $59 million. The exchange business is, in my view, in secular decline as the market has moved from fixed timeshares to more flexible VOIs, reducing the need to “trade” weeks or locations. That said, it still provides substantial cash flow while helping to diversify the business. Management has expressed an openness to selling the business given its long-term decline, but that is not my base case, as I see limited appetite for it at an attractive multiple. Instead, I believe it will be better for shareholders if TNL manages the decline and harvests the cash flow for capital returns.

The Balance Sheet Enables Capital Returns

Looking at its balance sheet, the company has $3.6 billion of corporate debt. This excludes the $2.1 billion of nonrecourse debt that funds its VOI receivables. Selling these VOIs into securitizations and retaining the residual interest to collect the remaining cash flow once bondholders are paid is a key way TNL generates cash flow. While delinquencies worsened a bit, this has not impaired this business. During the quarter, it completed a $325 million securitization at a 5.11% average coupon. This went to offset a maturity within the program, leaving the company with neutral adjusted free cash flow in the quarter.

Leverage remains in a strong position, at about 3.2x. This allows the company ample financial flexibility to return capital to shareholders. Shares now offer a 3.5% dividend yield, and I view this payout as secure. Beyond this, TNL has been aggressive in repurchasing stock. During Q1, it bought back $87 million of stock. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down 5.5% over the past year.

TNL Maintained Guidance

Alongside results, the company reiterated guidance of $1.03-$1.055 billion in EBITDA and gross VOI sales of $2.5-$2.6 billion. Management has not seen signs travel activity is slowing down despite these macro pressures, which should support continued strength in VOI sales. Plus, its business is much less cyclical than a hotel business since most of its revenue is recurring annually, namely timeshare fees, unlike hotels, which are more likely to see a drop in occupancy first if consumers pull back.

Considering the fact that Q1 beat guidance, reaffirming the full year implies a slightly weaker Q2-Q4 than previously. Now, Q1 is one of the seasonally quieter quarters, so management likely does not want to over-index to it. Beyond that, the exact fallout of the Iran War is still unknown, and a larger inflation spike could cause consumers to defer big-ticket, discretionary purchases like a VOI. As such, maintaining some caution makes sense, especially as there could be some upward risk in provisions. I do not view the decision to retain full-year guidance as management actively deciding to cut Q2-Q4 (though that seems to be the market’s impression) and rather view it as management maintaining a conservative stance amidst an uncertain macro environment.

The Sell-Off Appears Overdone

Back in February, I saw the company returning about $6/share to investors via buybacks or dividends, or about $400 million. Nothing in this quarter moves me from this view just yet. If anything, I see some upside risk to VOI sales, which can help to offset incremental provisions. Amidst this economic uncertainty, I believe maintaining conservative guidance is prudent, and some of today’s move likely reflects profit-taking after a large run. On the bright side, the pullback makes the buyback a bit more powerful, reducing the share count more quickly.

Given long-term growth of ~3%, I believe this is a business that should trade with closer to a 7% free cash flow yield, or past $80. Even haircutting this for delinquency uncertainty, I believe shares can gradually recover losses and return to $75+, as solid underwriting standards should limit the magnitude of the delinquency wave. I would use this pullback to add shares, recognizing it may require some patience to rebuild investor sentiment.

data.title: Underperformance Doesn't Mean A Bargain: Berkshire Hathaway Still Isn't Cheap
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.A, BRK.B) has had a rough start to 2026. Not only did it fail to outperform during the Iran conflict selloff like it did turning the tariff fiasco in April 2025, but it's failed to participate in the recovery as well. It has underperformed the index by 8% since my my last article in which I argued that it was still fully valued. The level of underperformance is a bit surprising considering Berkshire's announcement that it is repurchasing shares.

I sold my entire stake in early 2024 because of disappointment with Berkshire's acquisitions, which look even worse since I wrote the article two years ago. Since 2023, Berkshire dumped $16.5 billion into Pilot ($10.8 billion for the remaining shares, plus another $5.7 billion to pay off their high yield debt) for it to earn $190 million last year.

Since my sale, it's underperformed the index by 25%, and is now back to a more reasonable valuation compared to this time last year, especially considering the treasury rate is down to mid 3's and probably will head lower this year. Is it a Buy yet?

What's a reasonable expectation of future returns for Berkshire from here?

Whether this is a Buy hinges on what sort of return we can expect from Berkshire going forward.

I recently was in a discussion on Twitter/X where one person claimed Berkshire should return 10-13%/year from here (he gave no methodology for the numbers.) Another user, a smart investor, said 10%/year is reasonable. I don't think these are reasonable numbers, and 6-7% would actually represent good performance.

My estimate for Berkshire's 2026 earnings are to be flat with 2025, with some business growth offsetting headwinds in interest income and underwriting results. OxyChem should provide a bit of a boost. I think flat would be a very good result for Berkshire this year.

FY2025 results I believe are a good steady state without a lot of noise. BHE only had wildfire charges of $100 million versus far bigger numbers 2023-24, as should grow from here. MSR grew 4%, BNSF showed a big improvement. Underwriting showed an expected regression, and will likely regress further in 2026. Underwriting profits at the levels from the last few years are not sustainable long term (this applies to the entire industry, not just Berkshire. There's a reason Allstate (ALL) is trading at 8x and Progressive (PGR) is back to 12x.)

The current earnings yield from Berkshire's $44.5B in Operating Earnings is 4.5%. If we include the look-through earnings of the $300 billion at a weighted P/E of 18.7 (minus the dividends received from these companies), it adds another $11 billion, for an earnings yield of 5.6%.

The issue is the growth. Since 2023, almost all of the growth has been from insurance underwriting results, which are volatile and likely to mean revert, and higher interest rates on cash balances. As a group, the subsidiaries haven't done very well, and that's before considering the incremental investment.

Too much of Berkshire's already slow growth is inorganic

Whether or not this 5.6% earnings yield is "good" depends on the growth rate of the business. After all, growth rate is primarily what determines an earnings multiple (yes, even for Berkshire.)

But there hasn't been much growth in the past 3 years: organic growth from the non-insurance operating companies has been ~1%. This is concerning for a number of reasons

  • This is nominal profit growth. In real terms, it's negative.
  • It's not all organic. To get these numbers required $37 billion in acquisitions: Pilot ($10.8 billion since 2023 + $5.7 billion to pay off their high yield debt), Cove Point LNG ($3.3 billion), BHE GT&S ($1.7 billion), Alleghany ($11.6 billion, which had operating companies that did $300 million/year in earnings pre-acquisition that were moved into MSR) and additional investments in annual tuck-in's between Iscar, Marmon, and Clayton homes.
  • Berkshire's CapEx for the last 3 years totaled $59.3 billion, well ahead of D&A expense of $38.8 billion.

So Berkshire has invested a cumulative $57 billion over the past 3 years in its operating businesses to generate ~1% of nominal earnings growth. Even if we exclude Pilot (since it earned practically nothing) and Alleghany (which is predominately insurance), it's still $35 billion of net investment for 1% growth.

An alternate view - what would the earnings yield be with less cash, and how do Repurchases play a role?

Berkshire has ~$370 billion in cash, or $324 billion in net cash (I still do not understand why Berkshire is the only company where we report gross cash and ignore the parent level debt.)

Let's say cash was $200 billion lower, as if it were magically distributed tax free. We'd lose $7 billion in earnings, and Berkshire's market cap would be $800 billion.

The earnings yield, with look-through, rises to 6.1%.

I believe this played a role in the decision for Berkshire to restart buybacks. Even if shares are fully valued, repurchases make sense comparing an earnings yield of 5.5-6.1% to treasuries at 3.5% (and likely heading lower.)

Conclusion

I didn't get to where I am today by going after mediocre opportunities. -Charlie Munger

I'm kicking the tires here again because, like Berkshire, I'm not thrilled earning 3.5% taxable in treasuries (I was OK at 5%+.) Earning 6% in a Berkshire seems like a good alternative, and over a very long time horizon it likely beats treasuries. But I think there is too much risk currently for that 6% return.

  • Results at GEICO could decline, perhaps substantially, driven by industry rate pressures.
  • Results at PCC could decline because of higher fuel costs in the airline industry. PCC’s pre-tax earnings increased 34.2% in 2025 and 24.4% the year before. Based on my math, since Berkshire gives breadcrumbs but doesn't just tell you what PCC earns, PCC earned $2.5 billion last year, up a billion from 2023. It accounted for all of the growth in MSR last year.
  • Lower interest rates.

Berkshire could have a hard time moving higher if the quarterly results are coming in below last year, which I see as likely.

I believe investors who think Berkshire returns 10% from here need to recalibrate their expectations. 10%/year means Berkshire is generating $100 billion in earnings/value accretion per year. The most generous treatment of everything: 100% credit for all look through earnings and valuing them "as if" they cleaned up their capital structure and held less cash gets you to 6%. Where does the other $40 billion/year come from? Every time I ask the question, I get answers that are a mixture of double counting and finance alchemy, specifically.

  • Berkshire earns 6.1% + 12% on their $300 billion portfolio = 10%. You can count the earnings from the portfolio (Berkshire's preferred method) or you can count the capital appreciation (GAAP). You can't count both. It's often investors that cite the overheated market as a reason to favor Berkshire but still assume Berkshire's portfolio will return double digits.
  • Berkshire earns 6% + 4% repurchase = 10%. Share repurchases are a reallocation of value, it does not create it. Berkshire earns what it earned, and they re-invest those earnings. They can buy more treasuries, Apple (AAPL), or repurchase shares, but doing the latter doesn't magically add to their return.

5-6% earnings yield with minimal growth, free cash flow far below earnings, fairly valued by most methods, with acknowledged problems to be fixed in the operating units just isn't a compelling story. Admittedly, I'm drawn to the underperformance versus the S&P 500 in search of opportunity, but I run the numbers and compare to alternatives and believe this needs to get far cheaper to be attractive. The only bull case I can buy into is "probably better than treasuries medium/long term" and that is an awfully low bar. Greg's performance as an operator and investor is far from proven, and it's unlikely he gets as much slack as Warren does if the underperformance continues.

Investors need to open themselves to the reality that Berkshire today is too large to outperform and has morphed into a poorly run conglomerate that should trade at a discount to the sum of its parts, rather than a premium.

data.title: TUI: Drop Following The Iran Conflict, Upside As It Abates
data.description:
There's no doubt about it that travel and leisure companies, during times of geopolitical stress, face a significant headwind. We've had the opportunity in the past month and more to see first-hand just how violent such a trend at times may be, and how the market reacts when a company that makes much of its business from travelling and resorts handles a geopolitical event.

TUI AG (TUIFF), since the time of my last article, has dropped 20%+, and has only recently started recovering, just as violently as it dropped when the war began. Today, as I am writing this article, the company is up about 15% in a single day. Why? Because two of its ships recently cleared the Strait, and it seems that the conflict is abating a bit.

While this sort of volatility causes many investors to lose their appetite due to the ups and downs, I encourage you strongly to see these moves as an opportunity. While I admit that an event like COVID-19 can absolutely cause massive disruption to a company like TUI (home ticker TUI1.DE), and the company, when it was in materially worse shape than it currently is, actually saw years of risk and underperformance, that is not the TUI that we have today.

Volatile companies like this can provide for some of the most superb investments that there are. If we posit that we're investing in a company that has a very solid balance sheet and who's business idea, barring the momentary disruptions and ups and downs, is solid and timeless (TUI does "vacation" - quite timeless if you ask me), then it seems quite clear that any downturn faced by TUI (provided that fundamentals are solid - which I now argue that they are) momentary in nature.

And if they are momentary, it's all a question of buying at the right price, and then rotating at the right price.

The current geopolitical trend has interrupted TUI's recovery. But because I don't view this geopolitical trend as permanent, or in fact even impacting to the degree justified by the company's drop, I consider TUI to actually be attractive here.

What I will be doing is reviewing the February 2026 report, the Q1 '26 earnings release in mid-May, where we'll see how much upside we can expect now in April based on those numbers (and conflict-adjusted assumptions).

TUI AG: The recent drop is not justified based on the company's upside and operational results

TUI's drop is a very interesting one. It means that the company dropped from a 6-7x normalized P/E to a 4-5x P/E normalized. While TUI is not a BBB-rated company, and while it's still "on the way back" in terms of yield and operations, this hotel, resort, and cruise operator is one of my "favorites" due to the low price coupled with the high operational upside at even just a base type of growth.

1Q26 results saw little difference in terms of upside here.

Prior to the Iran war, things were going swimmingly. The company confirmed EBIT guidance, saw excellent HEX trading momentum (strong order demand, good 2H start), with revenue at almost €5B on a quarterly basis, and an underlying EBIT of €77M. In short, the company is showing baseline profitability, which is exactly what we're looking for.

The M+A segment is showing upside as well - but the best upside was probably in cruises, which saw a 16% YoY growth, with a 98% occupancy, meaning almost every TUI ship that leaves the harbor is fully booked at this time. It's too early to say that the Markets + Airline segment is seeing a full turnaround - especially now with the Iran war, with fuel prices and other trends negative - but TUI has shown that it has the potential to not only turn this segment around, but to show excellent overall multi-year profitability and growth. The central region is already profitable, and the Northern regions is getting there.

How is the company going to get this segment profitable? I've been analyzing it for some time, and here's my view, clarified and crystallized. The use of its integrated "marketplace" provides packages and experiences to the entire audience, while targeting about a 3% EBIT margin with its airlines. It focuses on single-seat sales, upgrading the aircraft to a single model, reducing efficiency, carbon costs, and maintenance costs due to simplification. With a unique structure based on a one-stop shop for holidays, I believe that TUI can make this profitable. I'm happy to see that the company isn't looking to outsource its air fleet, but rather is going to work on optimizing, integrating, and making the entire operation work.

I believe that, given enough time and given the opportunity for debt reduction, the internal operational leverage and efficiencies will result in a TUI that will not only be profitable in a wide variety of market situations but can rise well beyond historical levels.

And insofar as debt goes, I think the company is doing it exactly right. Since last year, the net debt has improved even further, to where it is now below €3.7B, which is a €500M. On the financial side, the company has also gone ahead and redeemed some of its convertible notes early, and the starter dividend from 2025 is going to be paid. What this means is that the company's strategy is not only on track, it's ahead of its plan.

So, with the guidance for the company reaffirmed, how should we view what's been happening to TUI as a result of the recent conflict? How will the revenue estimate of 2-4% be impacted, and what about the 7-10% EBIT increase by the summer of this year?

Unfortunately, we have to be realistic. The group EBIT of about €1.4B for the year may not be achievable with the Iran conflict. The company provided as much in recent guidance a few days ago, which also came in to adjust all of the company's relevant numbers for the year.

The Q2 EBIT is expected to be positive at least, compared to a negative EBIT for the YoY period. The Iran war is not as big an impact as I expected it to be, with the company only estimating €40M hit so far. But insofar as the annual EBIT goes, the company is now expecting a range of €1.1B to €1.4B, depending on recovery and the length of the war. Because my own estimates previously before this announcement were at or about €900-€1.2B, I was maybe a bit too conservative for the company. However, TUI suspended revenue guidance, which is more in line with my expectations. The company has been forced to spend a lot of capital on repatriating its customers and passengers - over 10,000 of them in fact - and its 2 middle-eastern ships are currently suspended, meaning fixed costs but no income.

Despite all of these negative impacts and estimates, I consider only a small impact on earnings to be likely. We'll know a bit more in a few weeks, on 13th of May. However, Iran may not be as big an impact as expected for a few reasons.

First off, the company is diversified. The biggest impact was Turkey and Egypt, and most of those have normalized - and where they haven't, they can be pushed to western destinations. The combination of operational leverage with its solid balance sheet will keep TUI on track to meet positive longer-term goals, which means I consider the current impact insufficient for changing my target.

TUI - Upside remains, I say "Buy"

In my last article, I moved my price target, or PT, to €15/share. You might expect me to change the PT here as a result of this, but I won't. Before the drop, the company's PT was closing in on double digits, and I remind you that much of my position was bought below €4/share. It shows you how good valuation can be here, and what can happen.

Even at only a 6x P/E, which is ridiculously cheap for a company estimating 11-13% AEPS growth per year for the coming few years, the company sports a current upside of at the very least 24% per year. However, I believe the company is worth more. While a BB-rated company is not worth 15-17x P/E, I believe a 10-11x P/E is certainly in the cards. TUI is one of the largest and most significant tour and experience operators in the world. It caters, basically, to much of the Western and European world, and for that, I consider it severely undervalued.

Even just a 10x P/E comes to over 35% per year, and a more accurate target of about 11x P/E comes now to an upside of 50%+ per year - and that's just estimating a price level of €20/share.

Not only do I believe that €20/share is entirely possible, I believe it is likely over time. Remember, this would only require a 200-250% improvement, and this is a percentage movement that the company has already achieved. I further remind you that the company has traded at well over €50/share.

TUI is a volatile company, but following COVID-19, I believe the evidence is in that it is turning over a new leaf, wherein it's becoming much more stable and lower leveraged. I like what I see, I consider Iran only a momentary disruption and I, therefore, reiterate Buy at my previous PT of €15/share.

Risks & Considerations

I've mentioned before, this used to be a speculative ticker - it's no longer, but volatility in this company is, of course, a significant factor. I would argue that international competition with the same type of specifics is very slim, in particular when it comes to the vertically integrated competitors. The closest peers would be hotel chains like Marriott International (MAR) or cruises like Carnival (CCL), but I consider TUI to be different from them (as does the market). Even if we were to include them, TUI still trades at comparatively cheap levels, especially after the recent drop before the 2Q 2026 period. Both on the forecast level and on the peer level, I therefore consider TUI to be an appealing Buy here, and I don't see risks as a necessary hindrance for this.

TUI trades under the native ticker TUI1 in Germany, with ADR TUIFF, an ADR that's share-based, meaning €15/share translates to roughly $17.5/share for TUIFF. I remain long the company here, and I may add more shares to my position.

Thesis

TUI AG remains one of the most appealing long-term travel companies in all of Europe. Unlike many of its peers, it has survived and actually thrives. I believe the combination of the current cheap valuation and upside. The Iran war, which has short-term driven it down, does not change my overall thesis on the company here.

I still, as of 2026 even with the Iran war included, cannot rate TUI anything but a Buy here, despite it moving up to €7.2. The company's cash flows are "too good" long-term, and the company's turnaround seems to be working, and the current geopolitical instability doesn't change it. I'm also, as mentioned above, switching my PT to €15, a slight adjustment. Even if the Iran war adds a bit to the company's recovery time, this does not worry me.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on future valuation and forecasts.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of €15/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a Buy rating as of April 2026 following Q1 '26, and with Q2 '26 coming up.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Barings/MassMutual's Credit Expertise: The 'Intel Inside' All 4 Barings Funds
data.description:
Barings/MassMutual: Private Credit's "Pros from Dover"

I first wrote about Barings Corporate Investors Fund (MCI) about five years ago (link here), explaining how its sponsor/manager, MassMutual, had been among the top "private placement" investors for many years. "Private placements" was the term originally used for privately issued corporate debt (i.e., loans and bonds not originated by banks), which was a staple of the institutional investment market (insurance companies, pension funds, endowments, etc.) for decades, long before it became more available to retail investors and popularly known as "private credit."

The expression "Pros from Dover" was originated in the 1970 film M*A*S*H by characters Hawkeye and Trapper, two zany Korean War army surgeons who were as likely to use the phrase to talk their way into hospital emergency rooms as they were to crash exclusive golf clubs. The "Pros from Dover" term stuck and is frequently used to describe those with widely recognized, demonstrated expertise, like Barings/MassMutual.

MCI and its almost identical sibling fund, Barings Participation Investors (MPV), have definitely shown themselves, over many years, to be the "Pros from Dover" in the high-yield corporate credit world.

While included as "high-yield bond" funds in most closed-end fund listings, both MPV and MCI emphasize in their reports and presentations their broad experience in, and focus on, the "private credit" space. Here's how they express it:

MCI and MPV benefit from being part of the larger Barings North American Private Finance (“NAPF”) platform, which has over 30 years of experience and commitments of over $28 billion to private credit.

The NAPF platform has provided two primary benefits: Direct deal origination and credit underwriting. NAPF has served as the Lead or Co-Lead on over 80% of its originated transactions and has had a senior loan loss rate of 0.03% since inception. The benefit of being the Lead or Co-Lead lender is the ability to lead negotiations on terms and exercise influence over the credit agreement.

MCI and MPV continue to benefit from NAPF’s strong origination relationships with private equity sponsors. Every private placement investment in the funds' portfolios was directly originated by Barings via a sponsor without a financial intermediary, which means one hundred percent of the economics are passed through to investors.

MCI's Total Returns:

MCI was started back in 1971. Here is its total return, both measured on market price and on net asset value ("NAV") over the past 15 years. On its NAV (which is how traditional open-end funds, like those sold by Vanguard or Fidelity, are measured), MCI was #2 of 24 funds, #1 of 21, and #1 of 17 for the past 5-, 10-, and 15-year periods. On a market price basis, MCI was #2 of 24, #4 of 21, and #2 of 17 for the same periods.

(If hard to read, click on the chart, and it expands and becomes clearer; otherwise, open it here: MCI_TR_new.png)

[Note that Total Return equals the sum of the cash distribution a fund pays during the reporting period, plus the change in the fund's value from the beginning to the end of the reporting period. "Total Return NAV" measures that change in value in the fund's Net Asset Value (i.e., the underlying total value of all the assets that the fund holds). "Total Return Market Price" measures that change in value based on the market price of the fund's shares during the reporting period, not the underlying value of the assets in the fund itself. If the Total Return NAV is greater than the Total Return Market Price, it means that the fund's price discount must have increased and/or its premium shrunk. In both cases, it means the market gave the fund less than full credit for the performance of its underlying assets.

If the Total Return Market Price is greater than the Total Return NAV, it means the fund's premium must have increased and/or its discount shrunk. That means the market increased the price of the fund proportionately more than the performance of its underlying assets actually warranted.

Most long-term investors regard the Total Return NAV to be the more meaningful of the two, since it tells the real story of how much the fund management has actually increased or decreased the underlying value of the fund's asset during the reporting period. Most economists and market experts (including some Nobel Prize winners) believe that the market will ultimately adjust a fund's or any other asset's price to reflect its actual economic value (i.e., the amount of cash income it generates over its life), as this article (link here) explains.]

MPV's Total Returns:

Competing with MCI for those top rankings has been its younger sibling MPV, started in 1988, with the same management team and virtually the same portfolio. MPV's record, below, shows it as #1 of 24 funds, #2 of 21, and #2 of 17 for the past 5, 10, and 15 year periods, with total return measured on an NAV basis. On a market value basis, MPV was #1 of 24, #1 of 21, and #1 of 17 for those periods.

(If hard to read, click on the chart, and it expands and becomes clearer; otherwise, open it here: MPV_TR_NEW.png)

Similar... But Different !?!?

It's no surprise that MPV and MCI have been neck-in-neck for leadership of the high-yield closed-end fund sector over the years, because, as mentioned earlier, the two funds are almost identical, even though one (MPV) is about 17 years younger. Same management and almost identical portfolios. To illustrate this, here are the lists of the top 10 holdings of each fund:

MCI is on the left, and MPV is on the right. Not that it makes much difference, because nine of the 10 top holdings are identical.

"But if that's all true, and the funds are so similar," some sharp-eyed reader is probably saying, "then why are their total returns so different in some key time periods?"

It's a good question and goes to the heart of why discounts and premiums are so important in the closed-end fund market. Here are the premium changes for MCI and MPV over the past year:

If we take a look at the total return tables for MCI and MPV above, we note that the one-year performance, measured on NAV, of both funds was identical: 6.9% each for MCI and MPV, respectively. Yet when the accountants add in the change in each fund's market price in relation to its underlying NAV total return (i.e., calculate its total return on market price), we see that for the same one-year period, MPV had a robust total return on market price of 10.8%, i.e., almost 4% higher than its NAV total return.

Meanwhile, less popular sibling MCI, with the same underlying performance at NAV (a positive 6.9%), didn't even come close to breaking even in total return on market price, with a negative -9.4%, i.e., over 16% worse than its underlying NAV total return. Comparing the total return on market price for the two funds - MCI and MPV - we see more than a 20% difference for essentially the same underlying performance. What gives?

The difference is easy to explain. The market, for whatever reason, decided to increase MPV's premium from about 10% a year ago to currently about 17%. This extra 7% price premium, over and above the underlying performance of the assets inside the fund, explains why MPV's performance - in market price terms - is so much higher than its performance in terms of its NAV. (Closed-end fund net asset values are often approximations, especially for less liquid asset classes, so the numbers won't necessarily match exactly. But directionally, it clearly explains what is going on.)

Meanwhile, its sibling MCI had the opposite experience, in spades. Its underlying NAV total return performance, as we have seen, was a not terrific but respectable 6.9% for the past year (just like MPV's). Yet its price went from a premium of 23% a year ago to a recent reported premium of only 6%, a negative 17% drop. That -17% would account for the difference in total return of -16.3% between MCI's positive one-year total return on NAV of 6.9% and its negative total return on market price of -9.4%.

Why Did This Happen?

The closed-end fund market, in general, can be pretty idiosyncratic and not always totally rational. But my best guess is that the answer lies in MPV's and MCI's respective premiums a year ago. We just said that MCI's premium 12 months ago was about 23%. Now it's 6%. Meanwhile, MPV's premium was about 10%, and now it's up over 17%. Given the similarity in the funds, 12 months ago MCI's premium was about twice that of MPV's, so investors obviously saw an opportunity to choose the cheaper one, MPV, over MCI, either swapping out of MCI and into MPV and/or investing new money into MPV. Both are pretty thinly traded funds, so it would not have taken a huge volume of trading to move their respective premiums, "rewarding" MPV with a better total return on market price than its underlying performance on NAV actually "deserved;" while "punishing" MCI with a lower total return on market price than its underlying performance "deserved."

Now the funds have swapped relative positions from a year ago. MCI had been at a premium more than twice that of MPV's. Now MPV's premium is way more than twice that of MCI's. So are we likely to see a reversal of the switch in investor preference we saw a year ago? Time will tell, but that would be my expectation.

Both funds have quirky distribution schedules, as we pointed out in an earlier article, and historically have tended to delay announcing and paying their "first quarter" dividend until after their annual meeting in early May. This means their four quarterly dividends end up all being paid between June and early January. This leaves about a five-month gap that confuses investors new to the funds as well as closed-end fund data providers, who often list the funds as "not paying a dividend" during the period just before the annual meeting. (Check it out here on Seeking Alpha.) So, we don't know if any further pricing surprises await us before or after the annual meeting (and expected dividend announcement) scheduled for May 14.

Given how virtually identical the two funds are, MCI is clearly the better buy today and would have to be rated a "Buy" to MPV's "Hold," for investors trying to decide which to reinvest distributions in or put new money into and wanting to make the better-value choice between the two funds.

Additional Options

But before we rush out to buy more MCI, there may be some additional and possibly better current choices to consider for those of us who are fans of Barings/MassMutual and have a high regard for its deep bench and long experience in the corporate credit arena, but also consider both MCI and MPV too expensive at their current premiums.

The Barings North American Private Finance (“NAPF”) platform, which we mentioned earlier, services the entire Barings/MassMutual lineup of funds and other investment vehicles when it comes to private credit, secured lending, and structured credit. The lineup includes two in particular that are familiar to many of us and that we hold in our Inside the Income Factory Core Model portfolios:

  1. Barings Global Short Duration High Yield Fund (BGH) is managed by Sean Feeley, who is one of the members of the MCI and MPV management team. There's also additional crossover at both the portfolio management and executive/director levels. BGH has a fine record of performance, with almost as high rankings as its siblings for total return over the years: #5 of 24 for 1-year total return on market price, #4 of 24 for 5 years, #3 of 24 for 10 years, etc. Here is its overall record:

(If hard to read, click on the chart, and it expands and becomes clearer; otherwise, open it here: BGH_TR_new11.png)

In summary, BGH offers:

  • Same top-quality management as MCI and MPV
  • Solid performance record
  • 10.4%+ distribution rate (higher than MCI or MPV)
  • -4.9% price discount

While I have held and written about MPV and MCI personally for quite some time, I have currently switched my personal holdings and Core model portfolio holdings to BGH because of the better value, yield, and discount.

2. Barings Business Development Company (BBDC) is a business development company (BDC) that Barings took over in 2018 (previously Triangle Capital) and then added several other mergers to the mix (Sierra and MVC) to create what is the current BBDC, which has by now been totally integrated into the Barings credit platform. I find it a very attractive way to benefit from the Barings/MassMutual expertise and experience while also enjoying the higher yield (11.6%) and generous discount (-19%) that so many well-managed BDCs are offering these days because of the overly negative media attention that "private credit" and the BDC industry (that specializes in private credit) have attracted over much of the past year.

Here is BBDC's performance record:

(If hard to read, click on the chart, and it expands and becomes clearer; otherwise, open it here: BBDC_TR_newmmm.png)

BBDC's performance has really improved in recent years as some of its legacy (pre-Barings) assets have been worked out and Barings/MassMutual has brought its long-standing expertise to bear. Note that its total return on market price has been #4 of 48 for the past year and #7 of 43 for the past three years. The -19% price discount provides quite a cushion against any potential surprises, and the 11.8% dividend yield is 100% covered by net investment income. I also hold BBDC in my personal portfolio, as well as in our Core model portfolios, in addition to BGH, as mentioned above.

Bottom Line

The Barings/MassMutual credit platform is one of the best in the business. Traditional funds MCI and MPV demonstrated this for a long time but have become too expensive, especially with the recent "dueling premiums" situation between the two funds.

Fortunately, there are these two other Barings vehicles - closed-end fund (BGH) and Business Development Company (BBDC) that also have that "Intel Inside" as the computer firms used to say, but this time it's Barings/MassMutual's long history and experienced credit platform that's "inside," while offering very attractive yields, discounts, and prices.

data.title: Rivian: R2 Is Launching At Just The Right Time
data.description:
While the S&P 500 has quickly shed its wartime jitters and rallied back to all-time highs, there are still plenty of pockets in the stock market where caution abounds. Investors have backed away meaningfully from earlier-stage tech companies, limited not just to software names impacted by the "SaaSpocalypse" but the electric vehicle space as well.

Shares of Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN), the second-largest EV maker behind Tesla, have seen their share price fall ~10% this year, and the stock is also down ~20% below December peaks around $22. Meanwhile, the growth backdrop for this stock remains unassailable as we prepare for the launch of the R2 in a few months.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Rivian (RIVN) in November, when the stock was trading at just $13 per share. In retrospect, I wish I had loaded up on more Rivian stock. At the same time, I'm comfortable holding this stock through the R2 launch and am confident in further tailwinds ahead. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating for the company.

There are two major catalysts ahead for Rivian. The first is the company's upcoming Q1 earnings release, due on April 30. The second is the start of R2 pre-orders, which are expected to open in June. Stay long ahead of then.

Q1 Look-Ahead: Pay Attention To ASP Growth And Software Sales

As a reminder, the earnings releases for most EV companies are a less volatile event because companies pre-release delivery figures about a month before releasing financials. For Q1, Rivian delivered 10,365 vehicles and affirmed its earlier guidance calling for 62k-67k in deliveries in 2026 (inclusive of new R2 figures and representing a rapid 42%-59% y/y growth rate against FY25 deliveries of 42.2k). For reference, delivery trends in the past year are shown in the chart below:

For Q1 specifically, deliveries of 10.4k well exceeded expectations for just 9.9k deliveries in the quarter. We also find the fact that Rivian grew deliveries at 20% y/y versus 8.6k in Q1'25 to be exceptional after a well-known product transition.

Even though vehicle deliveries are already a known data point, there are several core indicators that investors should watch out for in Q1 results:

  • ASP trends. We're hoping for Rivian's automotive revenue growth to exceed 20% y/y growth in deliveries. This would indicate that average selling prices of Rivian vehicles rose in Q1. What this would signify to me is that the deliveries strength in Q1 is coming from higher-end models of R1T and R1S, who are unlikely buyers of R2.
  • Software revenue. Another core indicator is Rivian's trend in software and services revenue. In Q4, software revenue of $447 million grew at 108% y/y, now representing roughly one-third of the company's overall revenue. We note that Rivian has an innate advantage here: though Rivian vehicles are positioned as premium luxury rides, its Autonomy+ software is priced substantially lower at $50/month or $2,500 for an outright perpetual purchase. Tesla (TSLA), meanwhile, charges $100/month for its FSD (full self-driving) package and eliminated the prior option to make a one-time purchase for $8,000. The sharp growth in software revenue indicates strong attach rates for Autonomy+ that are also beginning to turn into a competitive advantage for Rivian.
  • Further commercialization of self-driving technology. Earlier in April, on the heels of Rivian's deal with Volkswagen, Rivian's CEO, RJ Scaringe, also noted his intention to approach more traditional carmakers for an opportunity to license Rivian's software. Adding more OEM customers for Rivian software would be a major, capital-light tailwind to the company's gross margin development.

While we don't expect Q1 to make major waves for Rivian stock, it should be a largely positive catalyst as we wait for the R2 Performance launch in June.

R2 Is Entering The Market With New Tailwinds And Favorable Competitive Dynamics

Broadly speaking, we don't expect Rivian to make any changes to its guidance for the year, which calls for 62k-67k vehicle deliveries in 2026, ahead of any demand data for R2. The R2 lineup is expected to follow a staggered release cadence, with the high-trim $58k R2 Performance launching in June. This will be followed by the $54k R2 Premium in late 2026, the $48.5k R2 Standard Long Range in early 2027, and finally the $45k R2 Standard in late 2027.

But in my view, the stage is set for Rivian to outperform its guidance and potentially raise its forecast in Q2. To me, the competitive landscape has shifted in Rivian's favor. Last year, many potential car buyers were still holding out hope that Tesla would fulfill its promise of a lower-cost entry vehicle priced around $25,000.

What we got, instead, are the 2026 Model 3 at $36,990 and the 2026 Model Y at $39,990. These were modest price drops that were more than offset by the loss of the $7,500 federal EV tax credit that expired in September 2025. These new 2026 Model 3/Y variants extinguished hopes that a cheaper Tesla model is on the horizon. Many Rivian bulls had feared that the R2 launch would be coming on the heels of a cheaper Tesla reveal, but this turned out not to be the case.

The price gap between Rivian and Tesla shrinks further when we consider customers who want full self-driving features.

Consider, for example, a $58k R2 Performance vehicle. Paying $50/month for Autonomy+ over a 5-year ownership period results in ~$61k in total cost before taxes and fees. Compare that to the $45k Premium trim (or similarly priced $57.5k Performance trim) for Model Y; adding 5 years of Tesla FSD at $100/month is an additional $6k in gross cost.

We note as well that all Rivian purchases come with a longer 2-month trial of Autonomy+, versus just 30 days of Tesla FSD at purchase. Rivian's software pricing, set at half of Tesla's and with an additional perpetual purchase option that is ~70% lower than Tesla's now-extinct $8,000 option, is helping the company to shrink its price gap to Tesla. Compares will get even better as we move into 2027 and the <$50k variants of R2 are launched.

Vis-à-vis gas vehicles, we note that the ~30% increase in gas prices since the Iran conflict began has made many more car buyers open to purchasing an electric vehicle, providing further tailwinds for Rivian that may drive a boost to its deliveries guidance later this year.

Risks And Key Takeaways

Of course, there are also risks on the horizon for Rivian. The company could bungle the R2 launch and be plagued with negative reviews and feedback. Also, Rivian's gross margins are still razor-thin; we don't expect a substantial upward correction in the company's gross margin profile until it is selling R2 vehicles at a substantial scale.

Stay long here and hold onto this stock ahead of the R2 launch.

data.title: Trulieve Cannabis: Preparing For Rescheduling
data.description:
Trulieve Cannabis Corp. (TCNNF) is again soaring with the cannabis sector on signs the Trump Administration is moving forward with rescheduling cannabis. Ironically, the stock is back to where the sector traded back in early October when cannabis stocks jumped on more promises of a move by President Trump. My investment thesis remains Neutral on the stock, preferring for investors to Buy dips versus chasing big rallies.

Regulatory Uncertainty
