The cannabis stocks are soaring on signs the Trump Administration plans to move forward with rescheduling cannabis from Level I of the Controlled Substances Act (CSA) to Level III. Trulieve Cannabis jumped nearly 20% on the news, but the initial media reports appear to mis-categorize the path forward.

According to Marijuana Moment reporting, the DEA plans to announce another administrative hearing to start the process of the reclassification. This is a multi-month process that got derailed under President Biden, not something that finalizes the rescheduling this week.

The reclassification move would strip the 280E tax requirement from plant-touching cannabis businesses and provide potential for the government to pardon past 280E tax debt. For now, the details are too limited to know the full financial impact.

On the Q4'25 earnings call, CEO Kim Rivers outlined her view on the financial benefits of the government reclassifying cannabis as follows:

... it removes the punitive tax burden of 280E, lifting pressure on state legal operators and allowing conversion from the illicit market to regulated channels. Finally, rescheduling sets the stage for much needed reform such as safer banking and eventual uplisting to U.S. exchanges.

Trulieve Cannabis expects to report Q1'26 earnings on May 7. The multi-state operator (MSO) operates 234 retail dispensaries and over four million square feet of cultivation and processing capacity in the United States, mostly focused on the Florida market.

The MSO reported 2025 revenues flat with the prior year at $1.18 billion with strong adjusted EBITDA of $427 million and a 36% margin. Trulieve Cannabis recorded 2025 cash flow from operations of $273 million and free cash flow of $229 million.

The big question with the likes of Trulieve Cannabis is the unknown tax implications, with the company recording a $668 million liability for uncertain tax position liabilities.

As a reminder, the 280E tax requirement disallows cannabis operators from deducting operating expenses from federal income tax. The companies are left with a large tax bill, in some cases more than the income before tax profits produced by the business.

Trulieve cannabis has been one of the more aggressive MSOs in tackling the unfair tax position. The company has challenged the tax code and filed amended federal tax returns claiming refunds.

Path Higher

Trulieve Cannabis is in a solid financial picture before taking into account any tax issues. The company has a cash balance of $256 million with debt at $232 million with a 9.4% interest rate.

The MSO forecasts a tough Q1 with revenues down at least low single digits. The consensus analyst estimates have revenues falling 4.5% to $285 million.

The key to the financial story is that Trulieve Cannabis is producing strong cash flows with a target of $250 million in 2026 and free cash flow of at least $165 million, though the numbers are down from last year. The MSO will spend more on capex this year due to opportunities in Georgia and Texas.

The stock has 196.5 million fully diluted shares outstanding with a market cap of just $1.6 billion. Trulieve Cannabis only trades at roughly 4x adjusted EBITDA targets around $400 million.

Trulieve Cannabis should definitely rally from here on the removal of the 280E tax burden. The big question is how much of the unpaid tax liabilities the company would end up owing and whether the reclassification actually occurs.

Just as with the rips in October and December on President Trump's Executive Order, the stock is best bought and owned on dips. The stock market likely gives back most of these gains once the DEA becomes fully involved in the reclassification process again and investors face the uncertainty of the regulatory body officially making the move.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Trulieve Cannabis is attractively valued outside of the uncertainty of tax liabilities. The stock likely slips back to the $6 level, where Trulieve Cannabis becomes a lot more appealing on the next cannabis reclassification rip, possibly one tied to the actual regulatory move by the DEA.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Stifel Financial: IB Leads The Way Despite March Dealmaking Snag
data.description:
Stifel Financial Corp. (SF) logged in a good quarter. They have countercyclical elements in their trading volume-based commission businesses, and their IB business is not of massive scale and therefore is capable of scrappily winning share and keeping an idiosyncratic growth rate. While there are still a lot of questions around the Iran War, we think that some of this strong performance should be able to continue in the currently opened books. We do expect sponsor markets and tech to be a weaker dealmaking area for advisory shops in the US, but this shouldn't matter too much to SF, which is indexed to typical mid-market categories like industrials, as well as some healthcare and depositories, though we have some concerns around those areas in the mid-market as well. In terms of business composition and profile, SF is quite similar to Raymond James (RJF). So are their valuations. Therefore, there isn't really a value angle here. While dealmaking is supposed to be recovering already from a March slump, we are a little concerned about the relative performance of the mid-market.

Results

SF logged a decent quarter. In the lending business, there's very little exposure to software, hence nothing related to the SaaS-pocalypse to worry about. Similarly, SaaS-exposed BDC and private credit also don't have much bearing on the SF business. Results were as follows:

There was 22.3% YoY growth in revenues. Transactional revenue, being a less vigorous growth area in general, was a drag on that figure. Their transactional revenues have a meaningful countercyclical or at least a pro-volatility component where the KPI here is levels of activity. Volatility in the later March period would have brought that about and lent a helping hand to revenues from this segment as markets clearly repositioned to a new geopolitical moment and a new situation for supply chains, still rather unresolved. Equity and bond markets seem to be shrugging off the latest headlines, with peace talks still uncertain though the ceasefire is prolonged for the moment. Volatility is coming down, so a surge in activity should decelerate developments here for the moment but could also come back in force if things go the opposite way of peace in the Iran War.

On the other hand, a calmer environment for markets will be helpful for the asset management division. The recovery in broad indices too should help grow the base for management fees. AM was already a lift up on the overall revenue growth this quarter, and acceleration should be visible. AUMs did stall among some AM players as of March, so we expect some relief.

For the IB business, which drove revenues, we have more ample commentary. Both capital raising and advisory did well. Capital raising would have been penalized by the volatility. This is a counter-volatility business for sure, with IPOs and issuances shelved from day to day depending on market performance and waiting on developments. We expect some deferrals, as issuers will likely want more definitive answers on the Iran War before proceeding. For advisory and other types of dealmaking, the excellent growth is in contrast to an observed fall in dealmaking activity towards the end of March, as bad as on Liberation Day last year. SF had bucked this trend, obviously, with the mid-market exposure, less leverage to high debt, high profile, large ticket, and capital cost-sensitive deals coming in clutch. The US mid-market - and SF is heavily indexed to the US over the rest of the world - is concerned significantly with industrials, healthcare, and depository institutions. Depository institutions and healthcare (including biotech, which has an exacerbating reflexivity issue) are cost-of-capital sensitive, so we expect some amount of pressure regardless of the reported recovery in dealmaking activity in the currently open quarter, though perhaps SF's emerging scale can buck that trend.

Specifically on the depository side, there’s a lot of uncertainty, and this uncertainty is impacting buyers. You read the press saying about $150 oil and interest rates may be rising and what happens to credit spreads, et cetera. I think that there’s a pause.

Q1 Earnings, RJ Kruszewski - CEO of SF

Cost of capital is at issue because of the reinflation concerns around the War. But the US focus probably protects them from acute supply issues of oil, where the US has the benefit of being self-sufficient. In terms of any sponsor exposure, geopolitical concerns and reinflation concerns that drive leverage could limit the vigorousness of their deferred return to market, so we don't expect that to be a major delta for results. Still, a generally accommodating dealmaking environment, particularly in key areas like industrials, consumer goods, and healthcare, and an idiosyncratic profile should permit more growth, and equity markets calmed in April should accommodate not just M&A but also capital-raising deals.

For interest-driven businesses, the upward translation of the yield curve could be an opportunity to transform funding sources into better-yielding loans, which they do retail and commercially through Stifel Bancorp. Interest on idle cash likewise can be used to squeeze a bit better yields out of the upward translation of the yield curve.

Bottom Line

It's a bit of a challenging environment. Some verticals are not liking the cost of capital questions, to be sure, including depositories and reflexive companies like biotech within the healthcare vertical. But on the latter point and for capital raising in general, markets are shrugging things off for now, though we aren't sure how long that can last if the Iran War doesn't settle down as is being expected by markets based on their rise. The idiosyncratic SF mid-market profile should be able to generate growth regardless, though, we'd expect. However, on valuation, we don't see much of an angle. The IB exposure in overall revenues is similar to RJF. Otherwise, they similarly have AM, sales and trading, and other financial advisory activities, including a bank. They are a fair comp in our view, and both trade just above 13x. So no relative value angle. Though assuming more growth that can outpace comp and generate operating leverage and profit growth, there may be an absolute case. 13x isn't much given growth. Still, we aren't heavily compelled.
